title: The measurement of partisan sorting for 180 million voters
core_conclusion: Using exact residential addresses of 180+ million registered US voters and a novel spatially weighted distance-inverse exposure index, the study shows that partisan segregation in the United States is pervasive and severe across all place types and densities. A large proportion of voters live with virtually no residential exposure to the opposing party. Partisan isolation is distinct from racial and ethnic segregation and persists even within cities and census tracts, demonstrating that residential sorting by party operates at the finest geographic scales.
why_it_matters: First individual-level national measurement of partisan segregation; demonstrates that 10% of Democrats have cross-party exposure below 0.05; shows sorting within neighbourhoods beyond compositional effects; disentangles partisan from racial segregation; links extreme residential isolation to threats to democratic governance and affective polarization.
why_nature: National-population-scale big-data social science with 180B dyadic relationships; genuine methodological breakthrough solving the checkerboard/MAUP problems in segregation measurement; crosses disciplinary boundaries (geography, political science, sociology) central to Nature Human Behaviour; rigorous validation via 12,221-voter survey near the theoretical accuracy ceiling.
added_value_to_my_research: Mobility-based partisan exposure index -> dynamic service-area delineation for R2SFCA by replacing static residential kernel with GPS-traced activity spaces
Partisan isolation as social-context covariate for R2SFCA demand weighting in health-care accessibility models and telehealth digital divide research
Geohash + PostGIS k-NN pipeline (Harvard Dataverse) as scalable feature-engineering backbone for individual-level neighbourhood composition in GeoAI explainability workflows
180M-voter exposure calibration dataset for agent-based Schelling-type model of partisan neighbourhood self-selection, transferable to health-facility access dynamics

Generalizing the distance-inverse k-NN exposure measure to multi-dimensional (income, race, age, party) spatial diversity tensor -> fine-grained spatial entropy decomposition for healthy-cities analytics
